Some polyhedra with icosahedral symmetry

Inspired by this thread by John Carlos Baez and this thread by J.M.

Use the UI widgets to define the scene and the mouse to rotate/zoom/move it.

Implementation Notes

  • The lighting of the scene should be improved while keeping the number of lights small, so that also low-end graphics hardware can handle them.
  • Only the edges used in the construction of the polyhedra and their faces are displayed. The implicit edges at intersecting faces are omitted. As a consequence, the great dodecahedron shows the same edges as the plain icosahedron. The same holds for their transformed versions.
  • While the dodecahedron and its fully transformed variant have planar faces (pentagons or pentagrams), the faces of the intermediate polyhedra are not planar. Thus there is no canonical way to draw these faces. My solution is to triangulate a pentagon around its center (which is computed as the average of the 5 vertices).

Geometry Notes

  • The icosahedron becomes a cuboctahedron with transformation parameter (1 - 1 / √5) / 2 ≈ 0.276 and an octahedron with (1 + 1 / √5) / 2 ≈ 0.724.
  • At (1 - 1 / √5) / 2 ≈ 0.276 the dodecahedron edges (without faces) become two intersecting tetrahedra. At (1 + 1 / √5) / 2 ≈ 0.724 the dodecahedron becomes an interesting figure with cube/octahedral symmetry.
  • Why these transformation parameters? Interpolating between φ and -1/φ with these parameters yields 1 and 0.
